cards.grammarNote:
**Hola** is used at any time of day, unlike English 'good morning/afternoon'
No abbreviated 'hi' equivalent — **hola** covers all casual greetings
The exclamation **¡** at the start is unique to Spanish punctuation

**por favor** literally means 'by favor' — a fixed phrase, always two words
Can be placed at the start or end of a request: **Por favor, dame el menú** or **Dame el menú, por favor**
**haz el favor** (do me the favor) is a more emphatic alternative

**gracias** is plural (literally 'thanks/graces') — always used in the plural form
**Muchas gracias** = thank you very much (many thanks)
From Latin *gratia* — related to English 'grace' and 'gratitude'
**querer** stem-changes e→ie: **yo quiero** · **tú quieres** · **él quiere**

**necesitar** is a regular -ar verb: **necesito · necesitas · necesita**
**Necesito** + infinitive = I need to do something: **Necesito ir** (I need to go)
Distinct from **querer** (to want) — stronger sense of requirement

**Me llamo** = 'I call myself' — from the reflexive verb **llamarse**
More natural in conversation than **mi nombre es**

Subject pronouns are often dropped — verb endings show who the subject is.
**Soy estudiante** and **Yo soy estudiante** are both correct — use **yo** for emphasis

Two forms of 'you': **tú** (informal, one person) · **usted** (formal)
**tú** → friends · **usted** → strangers, elders, formal settings
**usted** takes the same verb form as **él/ella**

**él** (he) has an accent mark to distinguish it from **el** (the)
Subject pronouns like **él/ella** are usually dropped in conversation — the verb ending makes the subject clear
**Él es** → often just **Es** unless you need to emphasize or clarify

**ella** (she) shares verb forms with **él** (he) and **usted** (formal you)
All three use the same conjugation: **trabaja, come, vive**
**ella** is typically dropped unless needed for emphasis or clarity

**buenas noches** is used both as a greeting and a farewell at night
**noches** is feminine plural — **buenas** agrees: **buenas** not *buenos*
**que duermas bien** (sleep well) uses the subjunctive — a soft wish or command

**¿Cómo estás?** uses **estar** (not **ser**) because it asks about a temporary state
**estás** = 2nd person singular informal · **está** = formal (usted)
Common responses: **bien** · **muy bien** · **más o menos** (so-so) · **mal** (not well)

**Mucho gusto** = 'much pleasure' — a set phrase used when first meeting someone
Also: **encantado** (masc.) / **encantada** (fem.) = delighted to meet you
Response: **igualmente** (likewise) or **el gusto es mío** (the pleasure is mine)
cards.englishPhrase: Nice to meet you, I'm Ana.

cards.grammarNote:
**De nada** literally means 'of nothing' — don't worry about it
Alternatives: **con mucho gusto** (with pleasure) · **no hay de qué** (there's nothing to thank for)
In Spain you may also hear **no hay problema** or simply **por favor**

Spanish usually drops 'it' entirely — verb endings and context carry the meaning.
**Es bueno** (it is good) · **Está aquí** (it is here)
**ello/lo** exist but are rarely used in speech

**nosotros** (masc. or mixed group) / **nosotras** (all-female group)
Spanish uses masculine plural as the default for mixed groups
**vosotros/vosotras** = you-all (Spain only) · **ustedes** = you-all (Latin America and formal Spain)

**ellos** (masc. or mixed) / **ellas** (all-female) — same masc. default rule as **nosotros**
Even one man in a group of women → **ellos**
**ellos/ellas** are usually dropped: **Comen juntos** is more natural than **Ellos comen juntos**

**este** (masc.) / **esta** (fem.) for 'this' · **esto** = neutral, for unknown or abstract things
**¿Qué es esto?** (What is this?)

**ese** (masc.) / **esa** (fem.) = that (near the listener)
**aquel / aquella** = that over there (further away) — a three-way distance system
**eso / aquello** = neutral forms for unknown or abstract things

**mi** (my, singular) / **mis** (my, plural) — possessives agree in number, not gender
**mi amigo** · **mi amiga** · **mis amigos** · **mis amigas**
Note: **mi** has no accent; **mí** (with accent) means 'me' as an object of a preposition: **para mí** (for me)

**quién** (singular) / **quiénes** (plural) — always carries an accent in questions
**¿Quién es?** (Who is it?) · **¿Quiénes son?** (Who are they?)
Without accent, **quien** is a relative pronoun: **la persona quien habló** (the person who spoke)

**qué** (with accent) = what? in questions · **que** (no accent) = that, as a conjunction
**¿Qué quieres?** (What do you want?) vs **Sé que quieres** (I know that you want)
Also used in exclamations: **¡Qué bonito!** (How beautiful!)

**cuándo** (with accent) = when? in questions · **cuando** (no accent) = when, as a conjunction
**¿Cuándo llegas?** (When do you arrive?) vs **Llámame cuando llegues** (Call me when you arrive)
The conjunction triggers the subjunctive: **cuando llegues** not *cuando llegas*

**dónde** (with accent) = where? in questions · **donde** (no accent) = where, as a relative
**¿Dónde está el baño?** (Where is the bathroom?)
Location always takes **estar**: **¿Dónde está?** not *¿Dónde es?*

**por qué** (two words, accent) = why? · **porque** (one word, no accent) = because
**¿Por qué no vienes?** — **Porque estoy cansado.**

**cómo** (with accent) = how? in questions · **como** (no accent) = like/as, or 'I eat'
**¿Cómo estás?** (How are you?) · **como tú** (like you) · **como arroz** (I eat rice)
Also used for identity: **¿Cómo te llamas?** (What's your name?)

Greetings by time of day:
**buenos días** (morning) · **buenas tardes** (afternoon/evening) · **buenas noches** (night)
Casually, just **buenas** works at any hour
cards.englishPhrase: Good morning! How did you sleep?

cards.grammarNote:
**buscar** is a regular -ar verb but watch the spelling in preterite yo: **busqué** (not *buskei*) — c→qu before e to keep the hard /k/ sound
**buscar** doesn't need a separate 'for' — it's built into the verb: **Busco trabajo** (I'm looking for work)

**perdón** = excuse me (apology or getting attention)
**con permiso** = excuse me (when passing someone or leaving)
**disculpe** = also common for getting a stranger's attention
cards.englishPhrase: Excuse me, where is the main street?

cards.grammarNote: **poder** stem-changes o→ue: **yo puedo** · **tú puedes** · **él puede**

**saber** is highly irregular in yo: **sé** (just one letter, with accent to distinguish from **se** the reflexive pronoun)
**saber** = to know facts · **conocer** = to know people/places
**No sé** is one of the most natural responses in any conversation
cards.englishPhrase: I don't know where it is.

cards.grammarNote:
**tener** is irregular in yo: **tengo**
Also used for physical states: **tener hambre** (hungry) · **tener sed** (thirsty) · **tener frío/calor** (cold/hot)
cards.englishPhrase: I'm hungry. Do you have water?

cards.grammarNote:
**hacer** is irregular in yo: **hago**
Used in weather: **Hace calor** (It's hot) · **Hace frío** (It's cold)

**comer** is the model regular -er verb: **como · comes · come · comemos · comen**
Meals can also be expressed as verbs: **desayunar** (to have breakfast) · **almorzar** (lunch) · **cenar** (dinner)
**¿Dónde comemos?** — subject pronoun dropped, nosotros implied by **-emos** ending

**gustar** works in reverse — the thing liked is the grammatical subject
**Me gusta el café** = coffee pleases me = I like coffee
Singular: **me gusta** · Plural: **me gustan** (e.g., **Me gustan los libros**)

Two words for 'but': **pero** (introduces a contrasting fact) · **sino** (introduces a correction: not X, *but rather* Y)
**No quiero café, sino té** (I don't want coffee, but tea)
**pero** is by far the more common and versatile of the two
cards.englishPhrase: I want to go, but I can't.

cards.grammarNote:
**y** → **e** before words starting with i or hi: **madre e hija** (mother and daughter)
**o** → **u** before words starting with o or ho: **siete u ocho** (seven or eight)

**o** → **u** before words starting with o or ho to avoid confusion: **uno u otro** (one or the other)
**o...o** = either...or: **o café o té** (either coffee or tea)
**ni...ni** = neither...nor: **ni café ni té** (neither coffee nor tea)

Spanish uses **tener** (to have) for hunger, thirst, and temperature:
**tengo hambre** (I'm hungry) · **tengo sed** (I'm thirsty) · **tengo frío/calor** (I'm cold/hot)
These are 'having' a feeling, not 'being' one

**sed** (thirst) is feminine: **tengo mucha sed**
Like **hambre** (hunger), it follows the **tener** pattern for physical states
**sed** also appears in the phrase **matar la sed** (to quench your thirst — literally 'kill the thirst')

**persona** is feminine regardless of the person's gender — **una persona simpática** even if the person is male
The personal **a** is required before a specific person as a direct object: **Veo a una persona** (I see a person)

**lugar** is masculine: **un buen lugar** · **el lugar**
**sitio** is a common synonym: **un buen sitio**
**lugar** also appears in the idiom **en lugar de** (instead of)
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know a good place?

cards.grammarNote:
**cosa** is feminine: **una cosa** · **la cosa**
Used in many common phrases: **ninguna cosa** → usually contracted to **nada** (nothing)
**¿Qué es esa cosa?** — **esa** (fem.) agrees with **cosa** (fem.)

Three levels of 'here/there' in Spanish:
**aquí** (here, near me) · **ahí** (there, near you) · **allí/allá** (there, far from both)
**Ven aquí** = Come here · **Está ahí** = It's there (near you) · **Está allá** = It's over there

**allí** = there (specific location) · **allá** = over there (more vague or further)
**ahí** = there near the listener
**hay** (there is/are) sounds like 'ai' — completely unrelated: **Hay un baño allí** (There is a bathroom there)

**muy** = very (before adjectives and adverbs): **muy bien** · **muy tarde** · **muy cansado**
Never use **muy** before a noun — use **mucho/a**: **mucho trabajo** (a lot of work)
**Está muy bien** uses **estar** — a temporary positive state, not a permanent definition

**adiós** = goodbye (final, like you may not see them again soon)
More casual farewells: **hasta luego** (see you later) · **hasta pronto** (see you soon) · **nos vemos** (see you)
**chao/chau** (from Italian *ciao*) is very common in Latin America

**agua** is feminine but takes **el** in the singular (begins with stressed 'a')
Adjectives still agree feminine: **el agua fría** · Plural: **las aguas**
cards.phrase: Un vaso de agua, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
Use **estar** (not **ser**) with food quality adjectives — they are temporary states
**La comida está deliciosa** ✓ · **ser** would imply a permanent quality, which sounds odd for food

**casa** is feminine: **la casa** · **mi casa**
**en casa** (at home) is used without an article — a fixed expression
**ser** vs **estar**: **La casa es grande** (the house is big — permanent trait) vs **La casa está cerca** (the house is nearby — location always uses **estar**)

**tienda** = store/shop (general) — feminine: **la tienda**
**La tienda está cerrada** uses **estar** — **cerrada** (closed) is a state, not a permanent trait
Compare: **La tienda es nueva** (the store is new — permanent characteristic) vs **está cerrada** (currently closed — temporary state)

**efectivo** = cash (literally 'effective/actual money')
**tarjeta** = card · **tarjeta de crédito** (credit card) · **tarjeta de débito** (debit card)
In Spain: **metálico** is another word for cash

**ayuda** = help (noun, feminine) · **ayudar** = to help (verb)
**Necesito tu ayuda** — note **tu** (your, no accent) vs **tú** (you, with accent)
**¡Ayuda!** alone = Help! (emergency cry)

**baño** = bathroom/toilet (masculine)
Regional terms: **servicio** or **aseo** (Spain) · **baño** is understood everywhere
Location always takes **estar**: **¿Dónde está el baño?** — never **¿Dónde es?**

**siempre** = always · **nunca** = never · **a veces** = sometimes · **normalmente** = normally
**tomar** here means to drink — **tomar** is used more broadly than **beber** in Latin America
**por la mañana** = in the morning (lit. 'through/by the morning')
cards.englishPhrase: I always drink coffee in the morning.
cards.phrase: Siempre tomo café por la mañana.

cards.grammarNote:
**nunca** usually comes before the verb: **Nunca llego tarde**
If placed after, add **no** before the verb: **no llego tarde nunca**
Unlike English, double negatives are grammatically correct in Spanish

**ir a** + infinitive = going to do something: **voy a comer** (I'm going to eat)
**a** + **el** → **al** (mandatory): **voy al mercado** (not *a el mercado*)

**venir** is irregular: **yo vengo** · **tú vienes** · **él viene**
**venir de** = to come from: **¿De dónde vienes?** (Where are you from?)
**entender** stem-changes e→ie: **entiendo** · **entiendes** · **entiende**

**hablar** is the model regular -ar verb:
**hablo · hablas · habla · hablamos · hablan**
Most -ar verbs follow this pattern exactly

**dar** is irregular in yo: **doy**
The command **dame** (give me) is extremely common: **Dame el menú** (Give me the menu)

**ver** is irregular in yo: **veo**
**A ver** (let's see) is one of the most common filler expressions in Spanish

**con** + **mí** → **conmigo** (with me) — a special fused form
**con** + **ti** → **contigo** (with you) — same fusion
**con** + **él/ella/usted** stays separate: **con él** (with him)
**Vine con mi amigo** — **vine** is the preterite of **venir**: yo vine (I came)

**sin** + infinitive is common: **sin saber** (without knowing) · **sin querer** (unintentionally — lit. 'without wanting to')
**sin** is the opposite of **con** — both are prepositions
**azúcar** (sugar) can be masculine or feminine depending on region: **el azúcar** or **la azúcar**

**para** = purpose or destination: **un regalo para ti** (a gift for you)
**por** = cause, exchange, duration: **gracias por tu ayuda** · **por dos horas** (for two hours)
cards.englishPhrase: This is for you. Thanks for everything.
cards.phrase: Esto es para ti. Gracias por todo.

cards.grammarNote:
**en** covers 'in', 'on', and 'at' in Spanish — context determines which
**en la mesa** (on the table) · **en casa** (at home) · **en México** (in Mexico)
**en** + transport: **en autobús** (by bus) · **en avión** (by plane) · **a pie** (on foot — exception)
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.
cards.phrase: El libro está en la mesa.

cards.grammarNote:
**de** is one of the most common Spanish words
**de** + **el** → **del** (required): **el libro del maestro** (the teacher's book)
Used for origin, possession, and material

**bueno** shortens to **buen** before masculine nouns: **un buen día** (a good day)
**Buenas** alone is a casual greeting at any time of day

**malo** shortens to **mal** before masculine nouns: **un mal día** (a bad day)
Also used in **estar mal** (to feel bad, to be wrong)

**grande** shortens to **gran** before any noun (masc. or fem.): **un gran hombre** · **una gran idea**
**gran** often implies greatness, not just size

**pequeño** (masc.) / **pequeña** (fem.) — adjectives agree with the noun they describe
**Una mesa pequeña** — adjective follows the noun (most Spanish adjectives come after)
Diminutive **-ito/-ita** can replace small: **mesita** (little table) · **momentito** (just a moment)

**mucho** before a noun: agrees in gender/number — **mucho trabajo** · **mucha agua** · **muchos amigos** · **muchas gracias**
**mucho** after a verb (as adverb): invariable — **Trabajo mucho** (I work a lot)
**muy** is used before adjectives/adverbs: **muy bueno** (very good) — never *mucho bueno*
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of work.

cards.grammarNote:
**un poco** = a little (used before **de** + noun or before adverbs)
**un poco de agua** (a little water) · **un poco cansado** (a little tired)
**poquito** is the diminutive — a very small amount, or used to soften a request: **un poquito más** (just a tiny bit more)
cards.phrase: Un poco de agua, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
**se dice** uses the impersonal **se** + third-person — literally 'how does one say?'
This pattern is everywhere: **se come bien aquí** (one eats well here / the food is good here)
cards.englishPhrase: How do you say 'thank you' in Spanish?

cards.grammarNote:
**ayudarme** = help me — the object pronoun **me** attaches to the infinitive
Alternatively, it can come before the conjugated verb: **¿Me puedes ayudar?** — both are correct
**Perdón** at the start softens the request — polite attention-getter before asking for help
cards.englishPhrase: Excuse me, can you help me find the station?
cards.phrase: Perdón, ¿puedes ayudarme a encontrar la estación?

cards.grammarNote:
**costar** is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **cuesta** (it costs) · **cuestan** (they cost)
**¿Cuánto cuesta?** = singular item · **¿Cuánto cuestan?** = multiple items
**¿Cuánto** agrees with context: **¿Cuánto es?** is also common at a checkout
cards.englishPhrase: How much does this shirt cost?

cards.grammarNote:
**lo siento** (I feel it) = I'm sorry
**perdón** → minor bumps or getting attention · **lo siento mucho** → genuine apologies

**hay** = there is / there are — from **haber**, used only in the impersonal third person
**No hay problema** = there is no problem
**No te preocupes** = don't worry (informal) — reflexive command; formal: **No se preocupe**
cards.phrase: No hay problema, no te preocupes.

cards.grammarNote:
**ser** = permanent or defining traits: identity, origin, profession, material, time
**Soy americano** · **Es médico** · **Son las tres** (It's three o'clock)
When in doubt: is this a permanent fact about the subject?
cards.englishPhrase: I'm American and she's a doctor.
cards.phrase: Soy americano y ella es médica.

cards.grammarNote:
**estar** = temporary states, emotions, location, and progressive tenses
**Estoy cansado** · **Está en casa** · **Estamos comiendo**
Location always uses **estar** — even for permanent buildings
cards.englishPhrase: I'm tired and I'm at home.
cards.phrase: Estoy cansado y estoy en casa.

cards.grammarNote:
**saber** = to know facts or how to do something: **¿Sabes nadar?** (Do you know how to swim?)
Irregular yo: **sé** · Not to be confused with **conocer**, which means to know people or places
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know what time it is?

cards.grammarNote:
**conocer** = to know / be familiar with people and places
**¿Conoces a María?** — notice the personal **a** before people
Irregular yo: **conozco**
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know a good restaurant here?

cards.grammarNote:
**decir** is irregular: **digo** · **dices** · **dice**
Command form: **dime** (tell me) · Appears in **¿Cómo se dice…?** and **quiero decir** (I mean)

**pensar** stem-changes e→ie: **yo pienso** · **tú piensas**
**Pienso que** and **creo que** both mean 'I think that' — **creo** is slightly more common in everyday speech

**esperar** means both 'to wait' and 'to hope'
**Espera un momento** (wait a moment) · **Espero que sí** (I hope so)

**mirar** = to look at / watch (directs attention, implies observation)
**ver** = to see (more passive, just perceiving)
**¡Mira!** (informal) / **¡Mire!** (formal) — common attention-getters, like 'look!' or 'see!'
Don't confuse with **buscar** (to look for) — completely different meaning

**salir** is irregular in yo: **salgo**
**salir a** = to go out to: **¿Salimos a comer?** (Shall we go out to eat?)
**salir de** = to leave from: **Salgo del trabajo a las seis**
cards.englishPhrase: At what time does the bus leave?

cards.grammarNote:
**volver** stem-changes o→ue: **vuelvo** · **vuelves** · **vuelve**
**volver a** + infinitive = to do something again: **vuelvo a intentarlo** (I'll try again)

**llegar** is regular but has a spelling change in preterite yo: **llegué** (g→gu before e)
**llegar a** = to arrive at: **Llego a las ocho** (I arrive at eight)
Also used in **llegar tarde/temprano** (to arrive late/early) — very common expressions

**hoy** = today · **ayer** = yesterday · **mañana** = tomorrow
**hoy en día** = nowadays (a fixed phrase)
Time adverbs in Spanish come after the verb: **¿Qué hacemos hoy?** — **hoy** at the end is natural

**mañana** has two meanings: tomorrow and morning
**Hasta mañana** = see you tomorrow · **por la mañana** = in the morning
**la mañana** (with article) = the morning · **mañana** alone = tomorrow

**ahora** = now · **ahora mismo** = right now (more emphatic)
**ahorita** is a diminutive form common in Mexico and Central America — it can mean 'right now', 'in a moment', or 'soon', depending on context
**ya** (already/now) overlaps with **ahora** in some uses: **¡ya voy!** (I'm coming now!)

**Hasta luego** = goodbye (literally: until later)
**luego** can also mean 'then' in a sequence: **Primero como, luego descanso** (First I eat, then I rest)

**ya** has several high-frequency meanings:
**ya terminé** (I already finished) · **¡ya voy!** (coming!) · **ya no** (not anymore) · **¡ya!** (okay! / got it!)

**también** = also/too (affirmative) · **tampoco** = neither/not either (negative)
**Yo también** = me too · **Yo tampoco** = me neither
**¿Y tú?** / **¿Y usted?** = And you? — a natural follow-up after stating your own view

**porque** (one word, no accent) = because · **por qué** (two words, accent) = why?
**¿Por qué no vienes?** — **Porque estoy cansado.**
Easy trick: the question form has the accent
cards.englishPhrase: I can't come because I'm tired.
cards.phrase: No puedo venir porque estoy cansado.

cards.grammarNote:
**tal vez** and **quizás** both mean 'maybe'
**Tal vez venga** (subjunctive — more uncertain) · **Tal vez viene** (indicative — more certain)

**¡Claro!** / **¡Claro que sí!** = Of course! · **¡Claro que no!** = Of course not!
**claro** is also an adjective meaning 'clear' or 'light-colored'

**Está bien** uses **estar** — 'okay/fine' is a temporary state, not a permanent trait
**Todo bien** = all good (a very common casual reply)
**No te preocupes** = don't worry — reflexive command; **preocuparse** (to worry) is literally 'to concern oneself'

**¿Qué tal?** is a casual greeting — common responses: **Bien** · **Todo bien** · **Más o menos** (so-so)
Also asks about specific things: **¿Qué tal el café?** (How's the coffee?)

**¡Vamos!** = Let's go! / Come on!
**¡Vámonos!** = Let's get out of here! (more emphatic, reflexive)
Both are used interchangeably in casual speech

**¿verdad?** is a universal tag question like 'right?': **Está bueno, ¿verdad?**
**¿no?** is even simpler and very common: **Hace calor, ¿no?** (It's hot, isn't it?)

**otra vez** = another time = again — a very common set phrase
**de nuevo** is a synonym: **¿Puedes repetirlo de nuevo?**
**volver a** + infinitive is the verbal way to say 'again': **vuelve a decirlo** (say it again)
cards.englishPhrase: Can you say it again, please?

cards.grammarNote:
**más** = more · **menos** = less · **más o menos** = so-so / more or less
**más** + adjective = comparative: **más grande** (bigger) · **más tarde** (later)
**más** has an accent to distinguish it from **mas** (an old literary word for 'but')

**despacio** = slowly — used as an adverb, invariable
**más despacio** = more slowly · **muy despacio** = very slowly
The opposite: **rápido** (quickly) · **deprisa** or **rápidamente** are also common
**¿Puedes hablar más despacio?** — a perfect sentence for language learners to memorize

**la cuenta** = the bill/check at a restaurant (feminine)
To ask for it: **La cuenta, por favor** or **¿Me trae la cuenta?** (Can you bring me the bill?)
**cuenta** also means 'account' (bank account) and appears in **darse cuenta de** (to realize something)

**menú** is a loanword from French — note the accent on the final syllable
**la carta** is another word for menu, especially common in Spain
**¿Me das el menú?** — literally 'Do you give me the menu?' — a natural informal request

**mesa** is feminine: **la mesa** · **una mesa**
**Una mesa para dos** = a table for two — **para** here expresses purpose/destination
In a restaurant: **¿Tienen mesa para dos?** (Do you have a table for two?)
cards.phrase: Una mesa para dos, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
**listo** with **estar** = ready: **Estoy listo** (I'm ready)
**listo** with **ser** = clever/smart: **Es muy listo** (He's very clever) — same word, different meaning depending on ser/estar!
**listo/lista** agrees with gender: **¿Estás lista?** (Are you ready? — to a woman)

**cansado** uses **estar** (not **ser**) — tiredness is a temporary state
**cansado/cansada** agrees with the subject's gender: **Estoy cansado** (masc.) · **Estoy cansada** (fem.)
Past participle used as adjective: from the verb **cansar** (to tire/wear out)
